1: <header>Carbon footprint of the globe artichoke supply chain in Southern Italy: From agricultural production to industrial processing </header>
Roberto Leonardo Rana a , Christian Bux b , Mariarosaria Lombardi

<list>a Department of Economics, University of Foggia, Via R. Caggese 1, 71121, Foggia, Italy 
b Department of Economics, Management and Business Law, University of Bari Aldo Moro, Largo Abbazia Santa Scolastica 53, 70124, Bari, Italy</list>

ARTICLEINFO|ABSTRACT Handling Editor: Maria Teresa Moreira Keywords: Globe artichoke Supply chain Greenhouse gases Carbon footprint Global warming potential Circular economy|"The globe artichoke represents one of the leading vegetables worldwide, accounting for a global production of 1.52 Mt in 2020, and Italy is ranked as the top producer with over 0.37 Mt. Under the environmental perspective, it is considered as a large greenhouse gases emitter, and its environmental sustainability, from agricultural production to industrial transformation, should be enhanced. The present research assesses the global warming potential of the globe artichoke crop in the Apulia region (Southern Italy) through the carbon footprint (CF) method (ISO/TS 14067:2018), and it evaluates the CO2eq emissions associated with input acquisition, artichoke cultivation and industrial processing. It also discusses strategies to reduce GHG emissions by optimizing the artichoke supply chain, according to a circular economy perspective. The main functional unit is 1 High-Density- Polyethylene (HDPE) drum of 229 kg, composed of 150 kg of drained finished ""rustic artichoke"" 70 kg of pre- serving liquid, and 9 kg of HDPE drum. Results reveal that the CF is 335.84 kg CO2eq, of which 276.86 kg CO2eq are generated during the agricultural production and 58.97 kg CO2eq during the industrial processing. CO2eq emissions could be reduced by increasing crop yield per ha, or by substituting chemical fertilizers with manure, PK3 fertilizer and bio stimulants. Under the circular economy lens, artichoke waste biomass could be used to produce: (a) bioenergy; (b) bio-stimulants and biomaterials; and (c) inulin. The research represents the first article, which assesses the CF of either the agricultural production or the industrial processing in the artichoke supply chain."
2: The Farm to Fork Strategy, as a key element of the European Green Deal, requires the enhancement of the sustainable agriculture to tackle climate change, to pursue sustainable development in the European Union (EU) and to make food systems fair, healthy and environmentally friendly (European Commission, 2022). Nowadays, globally, half of the habitable land (51 million km2) is involved in agriculture, and for the crop production, which excludes animal feed, more than 11 million km2 of arable land are used (Ritchie and Roser, 2021). Further, the intensive agriculture has a detrimental effect on the environment, not only in terms of consumption of limited natural and energy resources (Kir- schenmann, 2010; De Corato, 2020), but also considering the increasing release of greenhouse gases (GHGs). It is estimated that crops' produc- tion for human consumption generates over 21% of the entire GHG emissions, for an amount of approx. 2.8 Gt of CO2eq. In the EU, Italy represents the third highest GHGs' emitter, accounting for 418 kt of

CO2eq, soon after Germany and France (Poore and Nemecek, 2018). The transition to sustainable food systems is required under either the environmental or the economic perspective, since its implementa- tion provides new opportunities for each phase of the food supply chain by attracting more and more consumers on the global scale (Hsu et al., 2020). Consumers are interested in food products, which guarantee a neutral or a positive environmental impact, help mitigate climate change, reverse the loss of biodiversity, and preserve affordability while generating fairer economic returns (European Commission, 2022). However, achieving sustainable agriculture commands new strategies to be adopted, such as the measurement of the environmental impacts associated with agricultural production and industrial processing (Eu- ropean Environment Agency, 2019; Amicarelli and Bux, 2020), as well as the implementation of the circular economy paradigms (Gao et al., 2020).
Several tools based on the life-cycle-approach have been introduced in the environmental accounting to fight climate change, to chase

sustainable agriculture and to develop circular economy strategies (D'Amato and Korhonen, 2021). The environmental research in the agri-food sector applies the input-output analysis (Boylan et al., 2020), the material flow analysis (Amicarelli et al., 2021), the life cycle assessment (LCA) (Cabot et al., 2022), as well as the eco-balances and the carbon or water footprints (Rana et al., 2019). Among them, the carbon footprint (CF) has been applied with success to entire agri-food supply chains, from agricultural stage to industrial processing, to quantify the GHG emissions, to minimize their potential sources and to inform relevant stakeholders by identifying hotspots and suitable op- portunities for their improvement (Plassmann and Edwards-Jones, 2010). CF can be defined as "the amount of GHGs emitted during the production and the treatment of the food waste equivalent to the radi- ative forcing exhibited by the unit CO2 emitted" and expressed in kg of CO2eq (Tang, 2020). Further, it is able to identify the critical economic sectors where policy makers may intervene to implement energy effi- ciency measures to reduce environmental costs (Lombardi et al., 2017)

Among the different agricultural products, certainly the globe arti- choke [Cynara cardunculus L. var. scolymus (L.) Fiori] is ranked among the most produced vegetables, accounting for a total production of 1.52 Mt in 2020, and Italy is ranked as the top producer with over 0.37 Mt (FAOstat, 2022). Furthermore, under the environmental perspective, it contributes to the increase in GHG emissions, being its carbon footprint equal to 0.86 kg CO2eq kg-1 of fresh product (Martin-Gorriz et al., 2020) (more details in section 2.1 and 2.2.)

In the light of these premises, the present research evaluates the CF (according to the ISO/TS 14067:2018) (ISO, 2018) of the globe arti- choke supply chain in the province of Foggia (Apulia region in Southern Italy), considering that this area represents the Italian globe artichoke top producer, with 0.064 Mt (equal to 17% of the total national amount and 4% at the global level) (FAOstat, 2022; Istat, 2022). Specifically, the purpose of the study is to: (a) assess the global warming potential of the globe artichoke supply chain, by evaluating the CO2eq emissions deriving from the artichoke cultivation to the industrial processing, assessing the most impacting stage; and (b) to discuss strategies to reduce GHG emissions by optimizing the artichoke supply chain, ac- cording to a circular economy perspective.

To the best of the authors' knowledge, this research represents the first CF that assesses the global warming potential of both the agricul- tural production and the industrial processing of the globe artichoke supply chain. Thus, the novelty and originality of the present paper is the different and broader approach (enlargement of the system bound- aries) in estimating the GHG emissions of this specific supply chain. Nowadays, the CF calculation is becoming strategic for supporting the implementation of GHG mitigation actions (mainly emissions' reduc- tion) in the agricultural sector. This is in line with the Sustainable Development Goal no. 13 of the UN Agenda 2030 and, specifically, with the objective of the European Green Deal of zero GHG emissions by 2050. Such study is an initial stage of a wider research funded by the University of Foggia (Italy) on the environmental sustainability of the globe artichoke supply chain. In future, the authors will carry out a more comprehensive analysis for assessing the other risks associated with this supply chain by using the LCA methodology. Finally, the expected findings could either satisfy consumers' knowledge on sustainable food products or help food companies in achieving more eco-friendly per- formances and acquiring market competitiveness.

In the light of these premises, the present research evaluates the CF (according to the ISO/TS 14067:2018) (ISO, 2018) of the globe arti- choke supply chain in the province of Foggia (Apulia region in Southern Italy), considering that this area represents the Italian globe artichoke top producer, with 0.064 Mt (equal to 17% of the total national amount and 4% at the global level) (FAOstat, 2022; Istat, 2022). Specifically, the purpose of the study is to: (a) assess the global warming potential of the globe artichoke supply chain, by evaluating the CO2eq emissions deriving from the artichoke cultivation to the industrial processing, assessing the most impacting stage; and (b) to discuss strategies to reduce GHG emissions by optimizing the artichoke supply chain, ac- cording to a circular economy perspective.

To the best of the authors' knowledge, this research represents the first CF that assesses the global warming potential of both the agricul- tural production and the industrial processing of the globe artichoke supply chain. Thus, the novelty and originality of the present paper is the different and broader approach (enlargement of the system bound- aries) in estimating the GHG emissions of this specific supply chain. Nowadays, the CF calculation is becoming strategic for supporting the implementation of GHG mitigation actions (mainly emissions' reduc- tion) in the agricultural sector. This is in line with the Sustainable Development Goal no. 13 of the UN Agenda 2030 and, specifically, with the objective of the European Green Deal of zero GHG emissions by 2050. Such study is an initial stage of a wider research funded by the University of Foggia (Italy) on the environmental sustainability of the globe artichoke supply chain. In future, the authors will carry out a more comprehensive analysis for assessing the other risks associated with this supply chain by using the LCA methodology. Finally, the expected findings could either satisfy consumers' knowledge on sustainable food products or help food companies in achieving more eco-friendly per- formances and acquiring market competitiveness.
8: The globe artichoke belongs to the family of Asteraceae, the genus Cynara and the species C. cardunculus L. ssp., and it is most used arti- choke in the agri-food industry. The Cynara cardunculus L. ssp. includes, indeed, three botanical taxa: the globe artichoke (var. scolymus (L.) Fiori), the cultivated cardoon [var. sylvestris (Lamk)] and the wild

The globe artichoke flower head (also named head or buds, often referred to as capitula) is the immature inflorescence, which consists of the fleshy peduncle (the floral stem), the receptacle, or thalamus (the basal part of the head) and a variable number (30-60) of bracts. The external bracts are fibrous and are usually discarded, while the internal bracts are tender and make up, together with the peduncle and the receptacle, the "artichoke heart" that is consumed worldwide fresh/ cooked or processed (usually canned or frozen). The plant first produces the main flower head, then it first generates a large capitula (the main/ primary head) at the apex of the central stem and then it develops progressively on the lateral branches the medium and small heads (the secondary heads). The head assumes different shapes, as instance cy- lindrical, conical, ovoid, ellipsoidal, spherical and subspherical and can weigh up to 400 g. At flowering, leathery green bracts encase a purple- blue flower, which after pollination gives seeds (achens); the weight of 1000 seeds ranges on average from 30 to 70 g (Basay, 2022; Sekara et al., 2015).

Its biological classification distinguishes between: (i) re-flowering types, whose production is continuous from autumn to spring; and (ii) non-re-flowering types, whose production is exclusive of spring seasons. As regards artichoke classification according to its head morphology, although more than 40 cultivars could be distinguished, it is possible to identify the main cultivars, as follows: (i) "Violetto di Provenza" and "Violetto di Sicilia" (ii) "Brindisino"; (iii) "Tema"; (iv) "Catanese"; (v) "Spinoso sardo"; (vi) "Terom"; (vii) "Romanesco" (Calabrese et al., 2020; Sekara et al., 2015).

In the last decade, the globe artichoke production revealed a stable trend, and its production has been estimated at 1.52 Mt in 2020, whereas its highest peak has been recorded in 2017 with 1.66 Mt. Specifically, Italy represents the top producer, accounting for approx. 0.37 Mt (24% of the entire production), followed by Egypt (0.30 Mt, 20%) and Spain (0.19 Mt, 13%) in 2020. As regards the cultivated area destined to the artichoke production, over 166 X 103 ha have been documented by latest statistics (FAOstat, 2022), of which 38 X 103 ha (23%) in Italy, 16 103 in Egypt and 13 X 103 in Spain. Considering the 2020 Italian market, Sicily and Apulia regions have been the most productive areas, accounting for over 0.15 Mt and 0.12 Mt respectively, for an average yield of 10 t ha (Ismea Mercati, 2022; Istat, 2022). Table 1 illustrates the artichoke production in the six provinces of Apulia.

Overall, it emerges that the province of Foggia produces over 51% of the entire Apulian globe artichoke production, accounting for more than 6400 ha of harvested land (54% of the Apulian harvested land). Although Sicily, as a region, produces more artichokes than Apulia (+0.3 Mt the province of Foggia still represents the largest producer in Italy compared to Agrigento's one which is the second Italian artichoke producer (Istat, 2022).
Table 1 Apulian artichoke production by province.

Province|Total area (ha)|Total production (t)|Provincial production rate Foggia|6400|64000|51% Brindisi|3900|42 000|34% Bari|720|10 800|9% BAT|550|4100|3% Taranto|200|3000|2% Lecce|160|640|1% Apulia (Total)|11 930|124 540|100%
Notes: The provincial production rate has been calculated by dividing the total production in each province by the total production in the Apulia region. Data have been retrieved from Istat (2022).

13: <header>2.2. Literature review on the artichoke environmental sustainability </header>

According to Rana et al. (2022), who have conducted a systematic literature review on the environmental sustainability of the artichoke supply chain, five main research trends have been found, as follows: (i) the valorization of biomass into bioenergy (Pesce et al., 2017, 2020) or into biomaterials ((Parlato et al., 2022); (ii) the valorization of food waste into bioenergy (De Menna et al., 2018; Fabbri et al., 2014) or into bioproducts (Amoah et al., 2020); (iii) the adoption of sustainable crop management strategies to increase the yield productivity, such as the use of different fertilization rates (Ierna et al., 2012), of biostimulants (Elsharkawy et al., 2021) or of organo-mineral fertilizers (Ierna and Mauromicale, 2013); (iv) the use of the environmental impacts assess- ment approach to evaluate and reduce emissions; and (v) the adoption of sustainable irrigation water management to reduce the water con- sumption (Salata et al., 2022). In the next lines, the authors report the main outcomes related to the environmental impact assessment in the globe artichoke supply chain.

Recent studies have monitored the environmental impacts associated with the globe artichoke agricultural stage, developing case studies, laboratory experiments and methodological frameworks (De Luca et al., 2015). Among those monitoring the environmental impacts of various agricultural productions (including the artichoke), only Lo Giudice et al. (2014) has dedicated a specific research to the Sicilian globe artichoke production. It has focused on 1 ha of harvested land as functional unit and has included as system boundaries the pre-implantation of the field, artichoke implantation and harvesting, whereas the industrial process- ing has been not considered. As a result, it has been estimated that such a crop consumer more than 85 000 MJ of primary energy and generated approx. 2400 kg CO2eq ha-Superscript(1) of harvested land. Compared to other crops, artichoke is considered as great GHGs' emitter, since tomatoes release roughly 1645 kg CO2eq, cabbage less than 1290 kg CO2eq, cu- cumber quite 1270 kg CO2eq and spinach 1060 kg CO2eq (Yan et al., 2012). As evidence of this trend, Beltran et al. (2022) have estimated that artichoke, together with other over-irrigated horticultural crops in Spain, are responsible for over 70% of the entire N2O peri-urban agri- cultural emissions. Further, Martin-Gorriz et al. (2020) have estimated that artichoke cultivation has the most detrimental effect on the envi- ronment, considering its intensive requirement of fuel, pesticides, fer- tilizers and water to produce rather low yield compared to other crops in Spain. It has assessed its carbon footprint at 0.86 CO2eq per kg, as well as a water footprint of 0.96 m³ per kg during the agricultural production. Thus, it results that no research has assessed the CF of the artichoke supply chain yet, both in the agricultural stage and the industrial processing.

15: The research implements the CF of the "rustic artichoke" (i.e., 100% Italian artichoke heart product, processed as is fresh) in the Apulia re- gion, province of Foggia, according to the ISO/TS 14067:2018 meth- odology (ISO, 2018). CF is carried out estimating the overall amount of GHG emissions associated first with the cultivation phase of artichoke and after with its industrial processing. This assessment has considered the GWP which represents a measure that converts the direct and indirect GHG emissions into CO2eq along an entire period corresponding to 100 years (Rana et al., 2019). The potential climate change impact of GHG emissions has been estimated via multiplication of their mass by the updated GWP1 corresponding value, expressed in "kg CO2eq per kg of emission" (Table 2 According to the ISO/TS 14067:2018, the study is based on an attributional LCA approach (ISO, 2006a, 2006b) that includes also system expansion and substitution and it has considered the following GHGs: carbon dioxide (CO2), methane (CH4), nitrous oxide (N2O), hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs), per-fluorocarbons (PFCs) and sulphur hexafluoride (SF6).
Following the CF framework and the LCA approach (ISO, 2006a,
Kyoto Protocol GHGs and related GWP values (2021).

Common name|Chemical formula|GWP 100 value per kg emissions Carbon dioxide|CO2|1 Methane (fossil origin)|CH4|29.8 Methane (non-fossil origin)|CH4|27.2 Nitrous oxide|N2O|273 Hydrofluorocarbons|HFCs|4.84-146 600 Per-fluorocarbons|PFCs|0.004-12 400 Sulphur hexafluoride|SF6|25 200

2006b, 2018), the research has considered the subsequent framework: (1) goal and scope definition, followed by the identification of the functional unit and the system boundaries (Section 3.1); (2) life cycle inventory analysis (Section 3.2); (3) life cycle impacts assessment in terms of CO2eq emissions (Section 3.3); (4) results interpretation through the circular economy lens. Fig. 1 illustrates the CF framework.

As a general assumption, it should be noticed that an implicit sequestration approach was used for GWP10 calculation, thus ignoring the CO2 flux within waste biomass disposed outside of the harvested land. This resulted from assuming CO2-in as being equal to CO2-out and so, the related net flux is zero (Johnson, 2009). Therefore, the waste biomass disposal footprint could be considered as carbon neutral, because it amounts to zero or close to it. Finally, DLUC (Direct Land Use Change) was not taken into consideration because the surface invested for feedstock crops cultivation was already a cropland. In addition, ILUC (Indirect Land Use Change) was not considered because its evaluation is only based upon economic assumptions, which are typical of conse- quential LCAs, thus resulting as inconsistent with the attributional ones (Ingrao et al., 2015).
16: The research implements the CF of the "rustic artichoke" (i.e., 100% Italian artichoke heart product, processed as is fresh) in the Apulia re- gion, province of Foggia, according to the ISO/TS 14067:2018 meth- odology (ISO, 2018). CF is carried out estimating the overall amount of GHG emissions associated first with the cultivation phase of artichoke and after with its industrial processing. This assessment has considered the GWP which represents a measure that converts the direct and indirect GHG emissions into CO2eq along an entire period corresponding to 100 years (Rana et al., 2019). The potential climate change impact of GHG emissions has been estimated via multiplication of their mass by the updated GWP1 corresponding value, expressed in "kg CO2eq per kg of emission" (Table 2 According to the ISO/TS 14067:2018, the study is based on an attributional LCA approach (ISO, 2006a, 2006b) that includes also system expansion and substitution and it has considered the following GHGs: carbon dioxide (CO2), methane (CH4), nitrous oxide (N2O), hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs), per-fluorocarbons (PFCs) and sulphur hexafluoride (SF6).
Following the CF framework and the LCA approach (ISO, 2006a,

2006b, 2018), the research has considered the subsequent framework: (1) goal and scope definition, followed by the identification of the functional unit and the system boundaries (Section 3.1); (2) life cycle inventory analysis (Section 3.2); (3) life cycle impacts assessment in terms of CO2eq emissions (Section 3.3); (4) results interpretation through the circular economy lens. Fig. 1 illustrates the CF framework.

As a general assumption, it should be noticed that an implicit sequestration approach was used for GWP10 calculation, thus ignoring the CO2 flux within waste biomass disposed outside of the harvested land. This resulted from assuming CO2-in as being equal to CO2-out and so, the related net flux is zero (Johnson, 2009). Therefore, the waste biomass disposal footprint could be considered as carbon neutral, because it amounts to zero or close to it. Finally, DLUC (Direct Land Use Change) was not taken into consideration because the surface invested for feedstock crops cultivation was already a cropland. In addition, ILUC (Indirect Land Use Change) was not considered because its evaluation is only based upon economic assumptions, which are typical of conse- quential LCAs, thus resulting as inconsistent with the attributional ones (Ingrao et al., 2015).

18: The goal of the research is to evaluate the global warming potential of the artichoke supply chain in the Apulia region (province of Foggia) by assessing the CO2eq emissions associated with the "rustic artichoke" through the application of the CF, either considering the agricultural production and the industrial processing stages. Further, the research aims to identify the most impacting stage along the supply chain and to discuss strategies to reduce GHG emissions by optimizing the artichoke supply chain, according to a circular economy perspective.
19: In the artichoke supply chain's study, the main functional unit is 1 High-Density-Polyethylene (HDPE) drum of 229 kg, which contains 150 kg of drained finished "rustic artichoke" and 70 kg of preserving liquid, composed of water (83%), vinegar (10%), salt (6%), citric acid ascorbic acid (1%), whereas the HDPE drum weights 9 kg. In addition, two in- termediate FU have been considered, as follows: (a) in the agricultural stage, 1 ha of harvested land; (b) in the industrial processing, 1 kg of HDPE for the packaging production.
20: <header>3.1.2. System boundaries and supply chain phases </header>

The system boundaries of the research encompass the different phases of the supply chain: from the cultivation and the harvesting processes, included in the so-called "agricultural production", to the industrial processing stage of the "rustic artichoke", until the HDPE packaging production (Fig. 2), according to a cradle-to-gate approach. Specifically, it shows the macro-activities, the processes, the primary and secondary material inputs, and the primary and secondary outputs. As concerns the artichoke cultivation, although different propagation techniques could be distinguished on the global scale, such as the seed propagation in greenhouse production systems, or the vegetative prop- agation through ovules or "carduccio" directly implanted in the soil, the present research considers the last one (Mehmeti et al., 2022), since it

represents the most common propagation method in the Mediterranean area and in particular in the site under study (Riahi et al., 2017; Mar- tin-Gorriz et al., 2020).

As regards transportation, the research considers: (i) an EURO 3 light-duty truck (3.5-7.5 t) has been used for moving chemicals (N, P2O5 and K2O fertilizers), fungicides, insecticides and herbicides, from dis- tribution to the farm gate, for a distance of 10 km, as well as vinegar and salt, from producer to the industrial plant, for a distance of 30 km; (ii) a EURO 3 heavy-duty truck (7.5-16 t) for manure from farm to the agri- cultural field, for a distance of 30 km; (iii) a EURO 3 heavy-duty vehicle (7.5-16 t) for fresh artichokes from the agricultural field to the indus- trial plants, for a distance of 70 km; (iv) a EURO 3 heavy-duty vehicle (7.5-16 t) for leaves and by-products from the industrial plant to the animal farm, for a distance of 20 km; (v) a EURO 3 heavy-duty vehicles (16-32 t) for the HDPE drum from Molina de Segura (Spain) to Foggia (Italy) (2230 km). Finally, as concerns workers transportation, a EURO 4 passenger car has been used for moving them from home to agricultural field (5 km) and from home to industry (5 km), for an average distance of 10 km.

2: The inventory analysis has been carried out to measure the use of natural resources and raw materials, as well as the fuel required for transportation, in the artichoke supply chain and in particular to pro- duce one HDPE drum of "rustic artichoke" of 229 kg. In line with Tricase et al. (2017) and Rana et al. (2019), the research develops a clear sys- tem, which represents real experiences as accurately as possible without compromising its easy understanding (Rubinstein and Kroese, 2016).

Primary data have been collected among 15 farms (for an amount of approx. 400 ha of harvested land) located in the province of Foggia according to face-to-face in-depth interviews among farmers (Thompson et al., 2019). Farmers have been asked to fill out a checklist with in- formation related to the agricultural inputs, with the chance of asking researchers for explanations during the compilation. As regards the use of chemical and natural fertilizers, farmers have been asked to identify their active principles and their percentages. Hence, considering the

availability of the agricultural primary data, the inventory analysis re- fers to average data, and a sensitivity analysis has been carried out to evaluate uncertain values (Section 4.1.). Further, industrial processing primary data have been collected in one of the most representative companies in the province of Foggia, which is specialized in artichoke-based foods and processes each year from 5 to 7 kt of fresh product. Researchers have periodically visited farmers and the com- pany's employees, from April 1 to May 31, 2022, the main period of production and processing of the globe artichoke in Apulia. Addition- ally, they have also interviewed competent operators, technicians and managers. As regards secondary data, they have been collected in literature or extrapolated from Ecoinvent v3.1. database included in the SimaPro software (Ecoinvent, 2013). Secondary data have been considered to adjust primary data and adapt them to average realities, or to fill in data gaps (Chiriaco et al., 2017). Fig. 3 illustrates the life-cycle inventory for the artichoke supply chain. Details related to data sources are reported in Section 3.2.1 (Table 2), Section 3.2.2 (Table 4). and Section 3.3.3. (Table 5).

(2019), the research develops a clear sys- tem, which represents real experiences as accurately as possible without compromising its easy understanding (Rubinstein and Kroese, 2016).

Farmers have been asked to fill out a checklist with in- formation related to the agricultural inputs, with the chance of asking researchers for explanations during the compilation. As regards the use of chemical and natural fertilizers, farmers have been asked to identify their active principles and their percentages. Hence, considering the

availability of the agricultural primary data, the inventory analysis re- fers to average data, and a sensitivity analysis has been carried out to evaluate uncertain values (Section 4.1.). Further, industrial processing primary data have been collected in one of the most representative companies in the province of Foggia, which is specialized in artichoke-based foods and processes each year from 5 to 7 kt of fresh product. Researchers have periodically visited farmers and the com- pany's employees, from April 1 to May 31, 2022, the main period of production and processing of the globe artichoke in Apulia. Addition- ally, they have also interviewed competent operators, technicians and managers. As regards secondary data, they have been collected in literature or extrapolated from Ecoinvent v3.1. database included in the SimaPro software (Ecoinvent, 2013). Secondary data have been considered to adjust primary data and adapt them to average realities, or to fill in data gaps (Chiriaco et al., 2017). Fig. 3 illustrates the life-cycle inventory for the artichoke supply chain. Details related to data sources are reported in Section 3.2.1 (Table 2), Section 3.2.2 (Table 4). and Section 3.3.3. (Table 5).
4: Table 3 illustrates the input-output data inventory related to 1 ha of harvested land in Foggia, and it refers to the agricultural production, including either cultivation or harvesting. During the agricultural stage, the carduccio is manually planted. Therefore, neither energy nor fuels are required, whereas chemical fertilizers (N, P2O5 and K2O), as well as natural fertilizers (manure), are required along the cultivation process. Specifically, in the light of the farmers' answers, the liquid urea- ammonium nitrate solution, the phosphoric acid at 54% for agricul- tural use and the liquid potassium at 30% for foliar and fertigation application have been considered. In terms of pest management, the research has considered common insecticides, fungicides and herbi- cides. Farm management operations include mechanical fertilization, reservoir earthworks and other field operations (e.g., harvesting), as well as irrigation, which require diesel and electricity, respectively.

which takes place at the beginning of the industrial processing stage through an electric machine that selects between artichokes' calibers, from 6 cm to over 13 cm; (ii) washing and husking, during which the artichokes are manually washed and peeled to remove the external leaves, considered harder and not suitable for the production of the "rustic artichoke". Such leaves, which substantially represent about 60% of the entire artichoke, are collected and then addressed to nearby farms for feeding livestock. It follows that about 40% of the entire artichoke is represented by the so-called "artichoke heart", at the basis for the pro- duction of the "rustic artichoke"; (iii) cooking, during which the arti- chokes are boiled in slightly acidic water (0.18% ascorbic acid) heated with Liquefied Petroleum Gases (LPG) boilers; (iv) preserving liquid production, which consists in mixing, through electrical machineries, water, vinegar, salt, citric and ascorbic acids; and (vi) drums filling, which concludes the industrial process for obtaining the finished prod- uct. All ingredients accounting for <1% have not been included in the life cycle inventory.

which takes place at the beginning of the industrial processing stage through an electric machine that selects between artichokes' calibers, from 6 cm to over 13 cm; (ii) washing and husking, during which the artichokes are manually washed and peeled to remove the external leaves, considered harder and not suitable for the production of the "rustic artichoke". Such leaves, which substantially represent about 60% of the entire artichoke, are collected and then addressed to nearby farms for feeding livestock. It follows that about 40% of the entire artichoke is represented by the so-called "artichoke heart", at the basis for the pro- duction of the "rustic artichoke"; (iii) cooking, during which the arti- chokes are boiled in slightly acidic water (0.18% ascorbic acid) heated with Liquefied Petroleum Gases (LPG) boilers; (iv) preserving liquid production, which consists in mixing, through electrical machineries, water, vinegar, salt, citric and ascorbic acids; and (vi) drums filling, which concludes the industrial process for obtaining the finished prod- uct. All ingredients accounting for <1% have not been included in the life cycle inventory.

14: <header>3.3. Emission factors and carbon footprint assessment </header>

Table 6 illustrates the emissions' factors considered in the present research. These, associated with the material and energy flows in the artichoke supply chain, have been retrieved from peer-reviewed articles, as well as from the IPCC Emission Factor Database (IPCC, 2021). In the light of the IPCC (2006) framework, the direct and indirect emissions related to chemical fertilizers have been estimated using a crop-modeling simulation data and country specific factor, according to a Tier 2 approach. Further details are available at Chiriaco et al. (2017). Direct and indirect emissions of CO2, CH4, and N2O from manure have been retrieved from Ecoinvent v.3.1 (Ecoinvent, 2013), considering the subsequent activities: loading and spreading of solid manure; diesel fuel consumption; amount of agricultural machinery and the shed; and emissions from tyre abrasion during the work process (e.g., preliminary work at the farm, transfer to the field). The factor does not consider the amount of spread material, dust other than from combustion and noise. Emissions of CO2, CH4, and N2O related to diesel for transportation have been retrieved from Ecoinvent v.3.1 (Ecoinvent, 2013) and have been calculated according to the GWP10 values (Table 1). 17: In the light of the life cycle inventory and the CF assessment, it results that the CF for 1 HDPE drum of 229 kg of "rustic artichoke" is 335.84 kg CO2eq, of which 276.86 kg CO2eq are generated during the agricultural production and 58.98 kg CO2eq during the industrial processing. As regards the CF during the agricultural production, which includes cultivation and harvesting, it has been estimated at 9353 kg CO2eq per 1 ha, which means 0.75 kg CO2eq per kg of fresh artichoke.

Fig. 4 illustrates the CF results with reference to 1 ha of harvested land. The highest percentage of CO2eq emissions comes from nitrogen fertilizers' use, for an amount of 3294 kg CO2eq (35.22%) and from electricity for irrigation, which accounts for 2773 kg CO2eq (29.64%), followed by manure use, which accounts for 1750 kg CO2eq (18.71%), and diesel used for fertilization (446 kg CO2eq, equal to 4.77%). Other impacting activities are fungicides/insecticides' use, which generate 152 kg CO2eq (1.63%) and diesel used for field operations (163 kg CO2eq, equal to 1.74%). Overall, it appears that diesel consumption, either for farm operations or transportation, generates over 1170 kg CO2eq, namely 12.50% of the entire CF of the artichoke agricultural production, whereas the consumption of natural resources, included electricity for irrigation, amounts to approx. 87.50%.

Fig. 5 illustrates the CF of 1 HDPE drum of "rustic artichoke" during the industrial processing stage. Once fresh artichokes are available, 370 kg of fresh products must be transported from the farm gate to the in- dustrial plant. The estimated CO2eq emissions, which amount to 335.84 kg CO2eq, could be distinguished, as following: (i) 276.86 kg CO2eq (82.43%) related to fresh artichokes production, namely those GHG emissions released in the agricultural stage to produce 370 kg of fresh artichokes; (ii) 18.86 kg CO2eq (6.15%) related to the production of 9 kg of HDPE, including transportation from Spain to Italy (3.42 kg CO2eq); (iii) 13.44 kg CO2eq (4.38%) related to the production of vinegar required for the preserving liquid preparation; (iv) 11.65 kg CO2eq (3.80%) related to the fresh artichoke transportation, from the farm gate to the industrial plant; (v) 9.23 kg CO2eq (3.01%) related to the LPG used for cooking. The remaining quota, which amounts to approx. 5 kg CO2eq, includes GHG emissions related to salt used for preserving liquid, electricity for machineries, preserving liquid preparation and waste- water treatment, as well as diesel used for vinegar, salt, leaves and by- products transportation. It must be remarked that the production of a 9 kg HDPE drum accounts for 18.86 kg CO2eq emissions, of which 13.28 kg CO2eq are attributed to the use of raw materials (virgin or recycled HDPE), 0.89 kg CO2eq to electricity for drums' production and 3.42 kg CO2eq to transportation from Spain to Italy. If CO2eq emissions are allocated among artichokes' hearts (33%), leaves and by-products (66%) and second caliber artichokes (1%), the entire CF could reduce to 110.82 kg CO2eq, since 221.65 kg CO2eq are attributed to leaves and

kg CO2eq per 1 HDPE drum of 229 kg of <<rustic artichoke

by-products and 3.36 kg CO2eq with second caliber artichokes.

Fig. 6 compares the agricultural production and the industrial pro- cessing GHG emissions to produce 1 HDPE drum of "rustic artichoke" in percentage of the total amount of CO2eq emissions. It emerges that the most impactful stage to the environment is the agricultural stage, ac- counting for 82% of the entire artichoke supply chain CF, whereas the industrial processing generates approx. 18% of the entire GHG emis- sions. Specifically, the largest amount of CO2eq emissions derives from the chemical fertilizers use (30.71%), followed by electricity use (24.44%) and manure use (15.42%). Furthermore, also diesel for agri- cultural operations (5.61%) and diesel for transportation (4.69%) ac- counts for a great amount of GHG emissions, of which the largest quota is represented by the manure transportation, the fresh artichokes' transportation from the farm gate to the industrial plant and the HDPE drum transportation from Spain to Italy.

20: In the light of the life cycle inventory and the CF assessment, it results that the CF for 1 HDPE drum of 229 kg of "rustic artichoke" is 335.84 kg CO2eq, of which 276.86 kg CO2eq are generated during the agricultural production and 58.98 kg CO2eq during the industrial processing. As regards the CF during the agricultural production, which includes cultivation and harvesting, it has been estimated at 9353 kg CO2eq per 1 ha, which means 0.75 kg CO2eq per kg of fresh artichoke.

Fig. 4 illustrates the CF results with reference to 1 ha of harvested land. The highest percentage of CO2eq emissions comes from nitrogen fertilizers' use, for an amount of 3294 kg CO2eq (35.22%) and from electricity for irrigation, which accounts for 2773 kg CO2eq (29.64%), followed by manure use, which accounts for 1750 kg CO2eq (18.71%), and diesel used for fertilization (446 kg CO2eq, equal to 4.77%). Other impacting activities are fungicides/insecticides' use, which generate 152 kg CO2eq (1.63%) and diesel used for field operations (163 kg CO2eq, equal to 1.74%). Overall, it appears that diesel consumption, either for farm operations or transportation, generates over 1170 kg CO2eq, namely 12.50% of the entire CF of the artichoke agricultural production, whereas the consumption of natural resources, included electricity for irrigation, amounts to approx. 87.50%.

Fig. 5 illustrates the CF of 1 HDPE drum of "rustic artichoke" during the industrial processing stage. Once fresh artichokes are available, 370 kg of fresh products must be transported from the farm gate to the in- dustrial plant. The estimated CO2eq emissions, which amount to 335.84 kg CO2eq, could be distinguished, as following: (i) 276.86 kg CO2eq (82.43%) related to fresh artichokes production, namely those GHG emissions released in the agricultural stage to produce 370 kg of fresh artichokes; (ii) 18.86 kg CO2eq (6.15%) related to the production of 9 kg of HDPE, including transportation from Spain to Italy (3.42 kg CO2eq); (iii) 13.44 kg CO2eq (4.38%) related to the production of vinegar required for the preserving liquid preparation; (iv) 11.65 kg CO2eq (3.80%) related to the fresh artichoke transportation, from the farm gate to the industrial plant; (v) 9.23 kg CO2eq (3.01%) related to the LPG used for cooking. The remaining quota, which amounts to approx. 5 kg CO2eq, includes GHG emissions related to salt used for preserving liquid, electricity for machineries, preserving liquid preparation and waste- water treatment, as well as diesel used for vinegar, salt, leaves and by- products transportation. It must be remarked that the production of a 9 kg HDPE drum accounts for 18.86 kg CO2eq emissions, of which 13.28 kg CO2eq are attributed to the use of raw materials (virgin or recycled HDPE), 0.89 kg CO2eq to electricity for drums' production and 3.42 kg CO2eq to transportation from Spain to Italy. If CO2eq emissions are allocated among artichokes' hearts (33%), leaves and by-products (66%) and second caliber artichokes (1%), the entire CF could reduce to 110.82 kg CO2eq, since 221.65 kg CO2eq are attributed to leaves and

22: The sensitivity analysis is used to evaluate the significance of each parameter of the model on the system behaviour (Hoops et al., 2016) and it represents a technique, which assesses the effect of one or more inputs' uncertainty on the output value (Barahmand and Eikeland, 2022). The present study, according to Dalir et al. (2017), has carried out a sensitivity analysis to evaluate the effectiveness of some parame- ters on the variability of the CF due to the variation of some input values. Indeed, it has considered the minimum and the maximum values,

recorded during the data collection on the agricultural stage, and it has evaluated the CF in such scenarios. Fig. 7 illustrates the sensitivity analysis results. Further details are reported in Supplementary Material. Notes: Red numbers highlight the difference between the minimum and the maximum value in the sensitivity analysis.

In the sensitivity analysis, considering the same output value per ha of harvested land, the variation in the use of manure from 15 000 t to 35 000 t per ha results in an upsurge in the GHG emissions from 1050 kg CO2eq to 2450 kg CO2eq (+133%). Same significant results occur in the variation of the N fertilizer from 180 kg to 350 kg per ha, with an in- crease by +94% in GHG emissions. Impressive variations have emerged if the use of herbicides varies from 1 kg to 4 kg per ha, since the asso- ciated GHG emissions increase by +300%. As regards the CF of the agricultural stage, in the minimum scenario, the emissions could be estimated at 6678 kg of CO2eq, whereas in the maximum scenario at 12 028 kg CO2eq (+80%). The entire CF could be estimated at 256.66 kg CO2eq for 1 HDPE drum of 229 kg in the minimum scenario, whereas in the maximum scenario the CF could be estimated at 415.02 kg CO2eq (+62%).

Finally, even if the human labour is not usually considered in GHG balances (Valadares Galdos et al., 2010), in this section the authors have decided to include it as an additional energy input in the CF assessment. As outlined by Ordikhani et al. (2021), human labour represents an energy input in the artichoke cultivation, and it has been estimated at 35 h per 1 t of artichoke head at the farm exit gate (Mehmeti et al., 2022). Its emission factor is 0.7 kg CO2eq per h (Ordikhani et al. (2021)),
CO2eq emissions contribution to the entire CF
Fig. 6. CF of 1 HDPE drum of 229 kg of "rustic artichoke" comparing the agricultural production and the industrial processing (in percentage).
kg CO2eq per 1 ha of harvested land
Fig. 7. Sensitivity analysis for the CF in the agricultural stage.

and it results that 12.95 h of human labour is required to produce 370 kg of fresh artichoke. Hence, approx. 9 kg CO2eq are generated due to this input, less than 3% in the CF of the agricultural stage, and 2.5% in the CF of 1 HDPE drum of 229 kg of "rustic artichoke".

26: Three researches on the global warming potential of the agricultural stage could be compared to the present analysis, although with different methods, since Martin-Gorriz et al. (2020) applies the CF method, whereas Mehmeti et al. (2022) and Lo Giudice et al. (2014) assess the GWP through the LCA. Hence, no research has investigated the indus- trial processing stage so far.

It emerges that the CF estimated in the present study (8013 kg CO2eq per ha) is lower than the Spanish one (approx. 10 754 kg CO2eq per ha) calculated by Martin-Gorriz et al. (2020). Although both studies consider the artichoke vegetative propagation method, the main dif- ferences in results can be attributed to the diverse energy demand or pesticides and fertilizers' use, as well as the dissimilar water consump- tion or the soil characteristics. For instance, in Italy it has been accounted the use of approx. 350 kg of N fertilizer per ha compared to 240 kg in Spain, as well as the use of 35 000 kg of manure compared to 7500 kg in Spain.

Mehmeti et al. (2022), by applying the LCA, have estimated the GHG emissions of the artichoke agricultural production at 7262 kg CO2eq emissions per 1 t of fresh artichoke, which is not SO far from the present CF. The higher amount of the present CF is due to the involvement of the inputs transportation, not included in Mehmeti et al. (2022).

Lo Giudice et al. (2014) have estimated the Italian GHG emissions at 2460 kg CO2eq per ha of cultivated land. Such an amount, which is approx. one-third of the current study's results, is due to different fer- tilizers' quantities, as well as emission factors associated with trans- portation. For instance, Lo Giudice et al. (2014) account for an amount of 180 kg of N fertilizer, which is significantly lower compared to the amount used in the present research. Further, they record a lower diesel consumption for farm operations (-30%) compared to the present analysis, and do not account for electricity consumption.
The current research, in line with the previous studies, has high- lighted that the largest quantities of CO2eq emissions are associated with
the use of chemical and natural fertilizers, the diesel consumption for farm operations (machineries and fertilizations) and the diesel con- sumption for transportation.

To reduce the environmental impacts associated with the artichoke cultivation stage, some improvement solutions could be adopted. First, the CO2eq emissions could be reduced: (i) by substituting chemical fertilizers, specifically N fertilizers (emission factor: 12.43 kg CO2eq/ kg), with manure (emission factor: 0.07 kg COeq/kg); (ii) by increasing crop yield per ha. With reference to the latter aspect, Elsharkawy et al. (2021) have demonstrated that it is possible to adopt PK3 fertilizer and bio stimulants to increase the total productivity of the artichoke crops in Egypt. Among other strategies, the use of bio stimulants made from seaweed extracts could, at the same time, increase plant height and number of leaves per plant, reducing the dependence on agrochemicals and boosting more sustainable agricultural practices. Furthermore, another improvement solution could regard the substitution of diesel consumption with bio-methane, which has demonstrated a better envi- ronmental performance (Lo Giudice et al., 2014).

As regards the industrial processing stage, it emerges that the highest amount of GHGs are associated with HDPE drum production and transportation, as well as with artichokes' transportation, vinegar pro- duction for preserving liquid and LPG consumption for cooking activ- ities. One improvement solution could be the acquisition of HDPE drums produced in Italy instead of Spain, with the reduction of environmental costs associated with transportation across 2230 km. In addition, it is possible to reduce CO2eq emissions by substituting diesel and LPG with more eco-friendly fuels, such as bio-methane.

At current, waste biomass is addressed at feeding livestock, which has demonstrated to reduce the amount of CH4 contained in the animals' effluent, as well as to avoid the growth of anaerobic microorganisms, which cause gastrointestinal negative consequences (Jonova et al., 2018). Moreover, as estimated by Monllor et al. (2020), artichoke waste biomass in the dairy goat feedstock during lactation, added in a rate from 40 to 60% to the conventional feed, could increase their milk yield by 4%. Additional sustainable pathways have been identified in litera- ture, and could be implemented in the Apulia region, as following. Under the circular economy lens and considering that more than 245 kg of artichoke waste biomass (e.g., leaves, stems) out of 370 kg of fresh artichokes (60%) are generated to produce 1 HDPE of "rustic artichoke",

artichoke waste biomass (e.g., leaves, stems) could be used to produce: (a) bio-methane, which could avoid emissions by replacing fossil fuels if it is obtained in an anaerobic digestion plant fed by artichoke waste biomass generated in the "rustic artichoke" supply chain (Gominho et al., 2018; Pesce et al., 2017); (b) digestate, which could substitute manure; (c) compost teas (Villecco et al., 2020); (d) biomaterials (Par- lato et al., 2022); and (e) inulin (Cavini et al., 2022) and its derived biofuels (Lee et al., 2022). For instance, if 250 kg of leaves and by-products are used to produce bio-methane, equal to 21.75 kg of dry matter as illustrated by Lo Giudice et al. (2014) and Pesce et al. (2017), it is possible to reduce the entire CF by roughly 9 kg CO2eq, from 335.84 kg CO2eq to 326.84 kg CO2eq per FU. Moreover, the transformation of leaves and by-products into bio-methane allows the production of digestate, which could be applied as soil conditioner, further reducing the release of the GHGs into the atmosphere.

Although both studies consider the artichoke vegetative propagation method, the main dif- ferences in results can be attributed to the diverse energy demand or pesticides and fertilizers' use, as well as the dissimilar water consump- tion or the soil characteristics. For instance, in Italy it has been accounted the use of approx. 350 kg of N fertilizer per ha compared to 240 kg in Spain, as well as the use of 35 000 kg of manure compared to 7500 kg in Spain.

Mehmeti et al. (2022), by applying the LCA, have estimated the GHG emissions of the artichoke agricultural production at 7262 kg CO2eq emissions per 1 t of fresh artichoke, which is not SO far from the present CF. The higher amount of the present CF is due to the involvement of the inputs transportation, not included in Mehmeti et al. (2022).

Lo Giudice et al. (2014) have estimated the Italian GHG emissions at 2460 kg CO2eq per ha of cultivated land. Such an amount, which is approx. one-third of the current study's results, is due to different fer- tilizers' quantities, as well as emission factors associated with trans- portation. For instance, Lo Giudice et al. (2014) account for an amount of 180 kg of N fertilizer, which is significantly lower compared to the amount used in the present research. Further, they record a lower diesel consumption for farm operations (-30%) compared to the present analysis, and do not account for electricity consumption.
The current research, in line with the previous studies, has high- lighted that the largest quantities of CO2eq emissions are associated with
the use of chemical and natural fertilizers, the diesel consumption for farm operations (machineries and fertilizations) and the diesel con- sumption for transportation.

To reduce the environmental impacts associated with the artichoke cultivation stage, some improvement solutions could be adopted. First, the CO2eq emissions could be reduced: (i) by substituting chemical fertilizers, specifically N fertilizers (emission factor: 12.43 kg CO2eq/ kg), with manure (emission factor: 0.07 kg COeq/kg); (ii) by increasing crop yield per ha. With reference to the latter aspect, Elsharkawy et al. (2021) have demonstrated that it is possible to adopt PK3 fertilizer and bio stimulants to increase the total productivity of the artichoke crops in Egypt. Among other strategies, the use of bio stimulants made from seaweed extracts could, at the same time, increase plant height and number of leaves per plant, reducing the dependence on agrochemicals and boosting more sustainable agricultural practices. Furthermore, another improvement solution could regard the substitution of diesel consumption with bio-methane, which has demonstrated a better envi- ronmental performance (Lo Giudice et al., 2014).

As regards the industrial processing stage, it emerges that the highest amount of GHGs are associated with HDPE drum production and transportation, as well as with artichokes' transportation, vinegar pro- duction for preserving liquid and LPG consumption for cooking activ- ities. One improvement solution could be the acquisition of HDPE drums produced in Italy instead of Spain, with the reduction of environmental costs associated with transportation across 2230 km. In addition, it is possible to reduce CO2eq emissions by substituting diesel and LPG with more eco-friendly fuels, such as bio-methane.

At current, waste biomass is addressed at feeding livestock, which has demonstrated to reduce the amount of CH4 contained in the animals' effluent, as well as to avoid the growth of anaerobic microorganisms, which cause gastrointestinal negative consequences (Jonova et al., 2018). Moreover, as estimated by Monllor et al. (2020), artichoke waste biomass in the dairy goat feedstock during lactation, added in a rate from 40 to 60% to the conventional feed, could increase their milk yield by 4%. Additional sustainable pathways have been identified in litera- ture, and could be implemented in the Apulia region, as following. Under the circular economy lens and considering that more than 245 kg of artichoke waste biomass (e.g., leaves, stems) out of 370 kg of fresh artichokes (60%) are generated to produce 1 HDPE of "rustic artichoke",

artichoke waste biomass (e.g., leaves, stems) could be used to produce: (a) bio-methane, which could avoid emissions by replacing fossil fuels if it is obtained in an anaerobic digestion plant fed by artichoke waste biomass generated in the "rustic artichoke" supply chain (Gominho et al., 2018; Pesce et al., 2017); (b) digestate, which could substitute manure; (c) compost teas (Villecco et al., 2020); (d) biomaterials (Par- lato et al., 2022); and (e) inulin (Cavini et al., 2022) and its derived biofuels (Lee et al., 2022). For instance, if 250 kg of leaves and by-products are used to produce bio-methane, equal to 21.75 kg of dry matter as illustrated by Lo Giudice et al. (2014) and Pesce et al. (2017), it is possible to reduce the entire CF by roughly 9 kg CO2eq, from 335.84 kg CO2eq to 326.84 kg CO2eq per FU. Moreover, the transformation of leaves and by-products into bio-methane allows the production of digestate, which could be applied as soil conditioner, further reducing the release of the GHGs into the atmosphere.

32: The present study has estimated the CF of the globe artichoke [Cynara cardunculus L. var. scolymus (L.) Fiori] supply chain in the Apulia region (the top producer, along with Sicily, in Italy and in the world). This research represents the first attempt to assess the global warming potential of both the agricultural production and the industrial processing of this supply chain. Indeed, the GHG emissions' calculation has been associated with 1 HDPE drum of 229 kg, (i.e, 150 kg of drained finished "rustic artichoke", 70 kg of preserving liquid, and 9 kg of HDPE drum), typically produced and marketed by different companies that operate in this sector. This approach has contributed to reduce the gap into the academic literature, which lacks of the CF application in the artichoke industrial processing. The findings showed a CF equal to 335.84 kg CO2eq, of which 276.86 kg CO2eq are generated during the agricultural production and 58.98 kg CO2eq during the industrial processing. Actually, this study, in line with the previous studies, has highlighted that the largest quantities of GHG emissions are associated with the use of chemical and natural fertilizers, the diesel consumption for farm operations (machineries and fertilizations) and for transportation, as well as the electricity for irri- gation. Thus, from the sensitivity analysis, it is emerged that the increase of crop yield per ha or the substitution of chemical fertilizers with digestate, PK3 fertilizer and bio stimulants could reduce the CF. Addi- tionally, artichoke waste biomass could be used to produce bio- methane/digestate, bio-stimulants and biomaterials, as well as inulin. Therefore, this research provides best practices to farmers, food processing industries and policy planners for promoting or enhancing a more environmental sustainable artichoke supply chain. Moreover, this information could be used to develop specific product certification (carbon labeling) or marketing strategies (carbon neutrality) to improve the organization's reputation for acquiring market competitiveness. The future research could apply other assessment tools, such as the LCA methodology and the water or the nitrogen footprint, able to implement additional eco-friendly best practices and to provide a more comprehensive evaluation of all the environmental impact categories. This may overcome the current limit of this study concerning the only estimation of the GHG emissions.
